name: AWS Fundamentals
description: Core AWS: IAM, VPC, EC2/S3, regions/AZs and the shared responsibility model.

options: An Availability Zone contains several Regions
A Region holds several isolated Availability Zones
Availability Zones span many separate Regions worldwide
Both terms describe the same global boundary
questions.explanation: You pick a Region (e.g. eu-west-2 London); within it, AZs are separate facilities you spread across for resilience.

options: Connecting private networks across regions
Managing identities and their permissions
Storing files and other large objects
Collecting metrics, logs and alarms
questions.explanation: IAM controls who (users/roles) can do what on which AWS resources.

options: A role is always tied to one human person
They are two names for the same thing
A role gives temporary, assumed credentials
A user cannot have any policies attached
questions.explanation: Roles avoid long-lived keys — services and users assume them to get temporary credentials, which is safer.

options: A load balancer spreading incoming traffic
A managed relational database service
Object storage for files and other blobs
A fleet of resizable virtual servers
questions.explanation: S3 stores objects (files) in buckets, with high durability — not a filesystem or database.

options: A group of users sharing one password
A storage volume attached directly to a server
A stateful firewall at the instance level
A subnet carved out of the wider VPC
questions.explanation: Security groups control inbound/outbound traffic per instance and are stateful (return traffic is allowed automatically).

options: Both operate statelessly at the subnet edge
Security groups are stateless by default
SG is stateful per-instance; NACL stateless per-subnet
SG filters per-subnet only; NACL filters per-instance only
questions.explanation: NACLs evaluate each direction independently at the subnet boundary; SGs track connections at the instance.

options: Security is optional for internal workloads
AWS is responsible for securing everything
The customer must secure the physical hardware
AWS secures the cloud; you secure your part
questions.explanation: AWS handles the underlying infrastructure; configuration, data and access control are your responsibility.

questions.text: Why avoid using the root account for daily work?
options: It costs more per hour than a normal user
The root account simply cannot be used at all
It has unrestricted access; a breach is total
It runs noticeably slower than an IAM user
questions.explanation: Lock the root account away (with MFA) and operate through scoped IAM identities instead.

options: Having a large range of addresses
A route to an internet gateway
Containing at least one EC2 instance
Being given a memorable, friendly name
questions.explanation: A subnet is public if its route table sends internet-bound traffic to an internet gateway; otherwise it's private.

options: Rentable virtual servers in the cloud
A managed relational database service
A service that resolves domain names
Storage for files and other objects
questions.explanation: EC2 provides resizable virtual machines in the cloud.

options: A permission attached to an IAM user
Your own isolated virtual network in AWS
A bucket that stores files and blobs
A single virtual machine you rent
questions.explanation: The VPC is the private network boundary where your resources live, with your own subnets and routing.

options: A firewall protecting your instances
A friendly DNS name automatically given to your service
A static public address you keep and remap
Extra block storage for an instance
questions.explanation: Unlike an instance's default public IP, an EIP stays the same and can be remapped between instances.

questions.text: Access keys committed to a repo should be…
options: Rotated and revoked without delay
Kept, since Git encrypts them at rest
Left in place if the repo is private
Emailed around so the team can use them
questions.explanation: Treat leaked keys as compromised: revoke and reissue, and never store keys in code.

options: Managed relational databases
Functions that run without servers
Storage for files and objects
A network for content delivery
questions.explanation: RDS runs managed engines like PostgreSQL/MySQL, handling patching, backups and failover for you.

options: A long-running virtual machine you administer
A load balancer that distributes traffic
A managed relational database engine
On-demand functions with no servers to manage
questions.explanation: You upload code; Lambda runs it in response to events and you pay per execution, with no servers to manage.

options: A serverless function triggered by events
Block storage for an instance, tied to one AZ
Object storage replicated across all regions
A managed database with automatic backups
questions.explanation: EBS is like a virtual disk for an instance; it lives in a single AZ, which affects where the instance can run.

questions.text: What does an Elastic Load Balancer do?
options: Stores files and serves them to users
Runs functions in response to events
Manages users, roles, groups and their permissions
Spreads incoming traffic across many targets
questions.explanation: An ELB spreads requests over healthy instances/pods for scale and availability.

options: Storing large amounts of object data
Connecting and routing private networks
Metrics, logs and alarms (observability)
Managing identities and their access
questions.explanation: CloudWatch collects metrics and logs and can alarm/notify when thresholds are crossed.

options: A JSON document defining allowed actions
A subnet carved out of a wider network
A firewall rule attached to a subnet
A virtual server running your workload
questions.explanation: Policies attach to users/roles/resources and specify permitted actions, resources and conditions.

questions.text: Deploying across multiple AZs gives you…
options: A lower monthly bill for the same setup
Higher availability if one zone fails
Noticeably faster individual processors
More storage capacity for your data
questions.explanation: Spreading resources across AZs means a single data-centre failure doesn't take your app down.

options: A load balancer for incoming requests
A service that resolves domain names
A relational database you administer
A managed registry for container images
questions.explanation: ECR stores and serves Docker/OCI images, integrating with ECS/EKS and IAM.

options: Functions that run without servers
Storage for files and large objects
A firewall guarding your instances
Managed Kubernetes run for you
questions.explanation: EKS runs the Kubernetes control plane for you so you deploy workloads without managing masters.

options: DNS, with health checks and routing
Management of users and their access
Storage for files and other objects
Compute capacity for your workloads
questions.explanation: Route 53 resolves domain names and can route traffic based on health, latency or geography.

questions.text: If you STOP (not terminate) an EC2 instance, its EBS root volume…
options: Persists, so your data survives the restart
Is wiped clean but the volume remains
Is moved into S3 until you start again
Is deleted immediately along with everything on it
questions.explanation: Stopping keeps the EBS volume (you still pay for storage); terminating by default deletes the root volume.

options: Faster networking between your services
An automatic backup of your data
Extra block storage for your instances
A second sign-in factor beyond the password
questions.explanation: Multi-factor auth means a stolen password alone isn't enough to sign in — enable it especially on privileged accounts.

options: You store and retrieve files at scale
Private instances reach the internet outbound only
You run containers without managing servers
The internet opens connections to private hosts
questions.explanation: It provides outbound-only internet (e.g. for updates) to private subnets while keeping them inaccessible from outside.

options: Routing network traffic between subnets
Encrypting all of the data stored on the resource
Making your instances run measurably faster
Organising, controlling access and tracking cost
questions.explanation: Consistent tags let you group, filter, control and attribute cost across many resources.

options: Managing encryption keys
Balancing incoming traffic
Running serverless functions
Resolving domain names
questions.explanation: KMS creates and controls encryption keys used to protect data across AWS services.
